cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to answer this question. The question is asking about the findings of the World Bank study from 1974 regarding economic growth in some backward (developing) countries. The options are about who benefited from this growth. First, I'll need to refer back to the passage provided. Let me scan through it again. The relevant part is in the third paragraph. The passage states: "A major study by the World Bank in 1974 concluded that it is now clear that more than decades of rapid growth in developing countries has been of little benefit to a third of their population." Wait, the wording here is a bit tricky. The sentence says that the growth has been of little benefit to a third of their population. So if a third of the population is getting little benefit, that implies that two-thirds might be getting more benefit? Or maybe the phrasing is that the growth didn't benefit most people, with only a small part (a third) benefiting. Let me read the next paragraph for clarification. The next paragraph says, "The World Bank study showed that increase in GNP per capita did not promise important improvements in such development indicators as nutrition, health, and education. Although GNP per capita did indeed rise, its benefit came down to only a small part of the population." This reinforces the idea that only a small part (a third) of the population benefited from the growth.
